# Micronutrients, hydration and electrolytes

> *Needed in tiny amounts, but non-negotiable. Your task here is **recognition** — name
> the major vitamins and minerals and their broad roles, spot the classic deficiency
> patterns and who is at risk, and understand fluid and electrolyte balance well enough to
> advise. Not biochemistry, not dosing. We meet each nutrient once, at the level of "what it
> does, what happens when it's missing, and where to find it."*

## Micronutrients enable; they don't fuel

Unlike the macronutrients, micronutrients supply almost no energy. They **enable** the
reactions that keep us alive — as cofactors, antioxidants, and structural components. We
need them in milligram or microgram amounts, and a **food-first** pattern (a varied,
mostly whole-food diet) supplies most of them [@afn2021curriculum].

The vitamins split into two classes that **behave differently**:

| Class | Members | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| **Fat-soluble** | A, D, E, K | **Stored** in fat/liver → deficiency develops slowly, but **toxicity is possible** with megadoses |
| **Water-soluble** | B-complex, C | Largely **not stored** → need regular intake; excess is mostly excreted |

*(One exception: **vitamin B12** is water-soluble but the liver holds years' worth —
which is why B12 deficiency creeps up slowly rather than appearing within weeks.)*

You can often **see this behaviour in the structure itself**. Switch between the three
vitamins below: vitamin C is studded with hydroxyl (–OH) groups, so it dissolves in water
and washes out; vitamins A and D are mostly oily hydrocarbon, so they dissolve in fat and
are stored — the reason they can build up to harmful levels if over-supplemented.

<div data-island="molecule" data-src="/structures/micronutrients.mol.yml"></div>

::: {.callout-warning}
## "More vitamins = healthier" is false
Fat-soluble vitamins (notably A and D) can **accumulate to toxic levels**. Even
water-soluble vitamins, though mostly excreted, are not a licence for unlimited high-dose
supplements. For most people, **food beats pills**.
:::

Minerals and trace elements carry **signature roles** worth recognising: **calcium** (bone),
**iron** (oxygen transport), **iodine** (thyroid hormone), **zinc** (immunity and healing),
and **sodium and potassium** (fluid balance, nerve and muscle function).

## Fat-soluble vitamins: A, D, E, K

Because they are stored, these four run out slowly — but the flip side is that supplements
can build up and harm. Two of them (A and D) are the ones that matter most in practice.

**Vitamin A** is needed for **vision** and for healthy skin, lining tissues and immunity.
Its deficiency is the world's leading preventable cause of childhood blindness: the earliest
sign is **night blindness**, progressing through dry eyes (xerophthalmia) to corneal damage
[@whoVitADef]. It comes ready-made in **liver, egg yolk and dairy**, and as **β-carotene** in
orange and dark-green produce (carrot, sweet potato, pumpkin, spinach, kangkong) that the
body converts as needed. The "more isn't better" warning is sharpest here: high-dose
**preformed** vitamin A is **teratogenic** (the reason acne drugs like isotretinoin demand
strict pregnancy prevention), whereas β-carotene *from food* is safe because conversion is
self-limiting.

**Vitamin D** behaves more like a **hormone** than a vitamin: the body makes it in skin from
a cholesterol precursor using UVB, then activates it in the **liver and kidney**. Its job is
to help absorb **calcium** for bone. Deficiency causes **rickets** in children (bowed legs,
knock-knees) and **osteomalacia** (soft, aching bones) in adults. It is common even in sunny
Singapore — one healthy-adult sample found about **42% at risk**, higher in women, older
adults, and those who work indoors or cover up [@sgVitDeficiency]. Few foods carry it (oily
fish, egg yolk, fortified milk), so skin synthesis and fortification matter. Toxicity is a
**supplement** problem, not a sun problem: mis-dosed products can push blood calcium
dangerously high.

![Oily fish such as salmon are one of the few natural food sources of vitamin D. Photo: DanaTentis, CC0.](/figures/food/grilled-salmon.jpg){#fig-salmon fig-alt="A grilled salmon fillet on a plate." width="320" .lightbox}

::: {.callout-note}
## The sunny-country paradox
"Singapore is sunny, so nobody is short of vitamin D" is a myth. Indoor work, sun avoidance,
covering up and ageing all lower skin synthesis — deficiency is common despite the climate.
:::

**Vitamin K** is needed for **blood clotting**. Its memorable deficiency is in **newborns**,
who are born with low stores and whose gut and breast-milk supply little — which is why a
**vitamin K injection at birth** is routine, preventing rare but catastrophic bleeding
[@aapVitK]. Adults get it from **leafy greens** and fermented foods. **Vitamin E** is a
membrane **antioxidant** from nuts, seeds and vegetable oils; dietary deficiency is rare and
mostly seen with fat-malabsorption [@odsVitE]. Neither is a common toxicity concern, a useful
reminder that not every fat-soluble vitamin is equally risky in excess.

## Water-soluble vitamins: the B-complex and C

These are not stored (bar B12), so they need **regular topping-up** — and their classic
deficiency diseases are some of the most recognisable syndromes in medicine. You are not
expected to investigate or treat these; **recognise the pattern and the at-risk group**.

| Vitamin | Broad role | Classic deficiency | Watch in… |
|---|---|---|---|
| **B1** thiamine | Turning carbs into energy | **Beriberi**; Wernicke–Korsakoff | Heavy alcohol use; polished-rice diets |
| **B2** riboflavin | Energy metabolism | Cracked mouth corners, sore tongue | Poor diet; little dairy |
| **B3** niacin | Energy metabolism | **Pellagra** ("3 Ds") | Maize-dependent diets; alcohol |
| **B6** pyridoxine | Amino-acid metabolism | Neuropathy, dermatitis | Isoniazid (TB) therapy; alcohol |
| **B9** folate | DNA / cell division | **Neural-tube defects**; anaemia | Pregnancy; alcohol |
| **B12** cobalamin | DNA + nerve myelin | Anaemia + **nerve damage** | Vegans; older adults; metformin |
| **C** ascorbic acid | Collagen; iron absorption | **Scurvy** | Very restricted diets; the isolated elderly |

::: {.callout-note}
## Two classic diseases of "missing" B-vitamins
**Beriberi (B1)** appears where the staple is heavily refined — historically, the switch to
**polished white rice** triggered epidemics across Asia, because milling strips the
thiamine-rich bran. It shows up as heart failure and oedema ("wet") or nerve damage ("dry"),
and in heavy drinkers as **Wernicke's encephalopathy** (confusion, unsteadiness, eye-movement
problems) — a give-thiamine-first emergency [@statpearlsThiamine]. **Pellagra (B3)** is
remembered as the **"3 Ds": dermatitis** (a sun-exposed rash), **diarrhoea** and **dementia**
[@statpearlsNiacin]. Both are stories about *how food is processed* changing what disease a
population gets.
:::

::: {.callout-note}
## Folate before pregnancy — a public-health win
The neural tube closes about **four weeks** after conception — often *before* a woman knows
she is pregnant. Too little **folate** at that moment risks **spina bifida** and other
neural-tube defects, so the advice is to have enough **before** conception, not after: women
who could become pregnant are advised to take **folic acid** periconceptionally
[@whoFolate]. Many countries now **fortify flour** with folic acid and have seen neural-tube
defects fall — the same "change the food supply" logic as salt reduction. **Singapore** does
not mandate this fortification [@ffiFortification], so the periconceptional advice carries more
of the weight here. Food sources live
up to the name (Latin *folium*, leaf): **dark leafy greens**, beans and legumes.
:::

::: {.callout-warning}
## Why B12 deficiency is dangerous to miss
Vitamin **B12** does two jobs — making blood cells *and* maintaining the **myelin** around
nerves. Deficiency therefore causes not only anaemia but **nerve damage** (numbness, pins and
needles, an unsteady gait) that can become **permanent** [@scdB12]. The trap: giving **folate**
can fix the anaemia while the nerve damage silently continues — so the two must be thought of
together. Watch **vegans and strict vegetarians** (B12 is essentially absent from plant
foods), **older adults**, and people on **long-term metformin** [@metforminB12]. Reliable
plant-based intake needs **fortified foods or a supplement** — spirulina and algae do *not*
count (they contain an inactive look-alike).
:::

**Vitamin C** builds **collagen** — the body's "glue" — so its deficiency, **scurvy**, is a
disease of things falling apart: **bleeding, spongy gums**, easy bruising, poor wound healing
and corkscrew-shaped hairs [@dermnetScurvy]. It was the classic disease of sailors, and the
first-ever controlled dietary trial (Lind's citrus, 1747) is why British sailors became
"limeys." Its second job is important here: vitamin C **greatly boosts absorption of
plant (non-haem) iron** — a squeeze of lime or a vitamin-C-rich fruit with an iron-rich meal
genuinely helps (see iron, below).

![Citrus and other fruit are rich in vitamin C, which also helps the gut absorb plant iron. Photo: Scott Bauer, USDA (public domain).](/figures/food/citrus-fruits.jpg){#fig-citrus fig-alt="Sliced oranges, lemons and other citrus fruit." width="320" .lightbox}

::: {.callout-note}
## Does vitamin C prevent colds?
Mostly no. Routine vitamin C **does not stop you catching colds** in the general population;
it only slightly shortens how long they last [@cochraneVitC]. And "water-soluble" doesn't
mean "harmless in any dose" — very high intakes of B6 (nerve damage) or vitamin C (kidney
stones) can cause harm. The point is *regular intake*, not *megadoses*.
:::

## Minerals and trace elements

### Iron — and why *where it comes from* matters

Iron is the oxygen-carrying core of **haemoglobin**. Its deficiency — tiredness, pallor,
breathlessness — is covered in the case below; here the key idea is
**bioavailability**: not all dietary iron is absorbed equally.

- **Haem iron** (from meat, fish, poultry) is **well absorbed** and little affected by the
  rest of the meal.
- **Non-haem iron** (from plants, eggs, fortified foods — the *only* source for vegetarians)
  is **poorly and variably absorbed**, and swings with what else is on the plate
  [@odsIron].

That makes **the company iron keeps** matter:

| Helps non-haem iron in | Blocks non-haem iron |
|---|---|
| **Vitamin C** (fruit, vegetables, a squeeze of lime) | **Tea and coffee** (tannins) — with a meal they can cut absorption by more than half [@hurrell1999tea] |
| A little **meat/fish** in the same meal | **Phytates** in wholegrains, legumes, nuts |
| Soaking, sprouting or **fermenting** (idli, dosa, sourdough) | **Calcium** (e.g. a big glass of milk with the meal) |

The practical, food-first advice: **pair plant iron with a vitamin-C food, and drink tea or
coffee between meals rather than with them.** Because plant iron is absorbed less well,
vegetarians and vegans need roughly **1.8× as much** iron and deserve a closer look. Signs
worth recognising beyond fatigue include **spoon-shaped nails (koilonychia)** and **pica**
(craving non-foods, classically ice).

![Red meat is the richest source of well-absorbed haem iron; plant iron needs more help to absorb. Photo: Hannahdownes, CC BY-SA 4.0.](/figures/food/red-meat.jpg){#fig-redmeat fig-alt="A cooked portion of red meat on a plate." width="300" .lightbox}

### Calcium and bone

Over **99%** of the body's calcium is in **bone**, which acts as a "bank": you make deposits
early in life (about **90% of peak bone mass is laid down by age ~18–20**, the account closing
around age 30) and draw on it later [@nofPeakBoneMass2016]. Adequate calcium, **vitamin D**
(needed to absorb it) and weight-bearing activity in youth build the reserve that protects
against fractures decades later — and hip-fracture rates in Singapore are **among the highest
in Asia**. Diet matters locally: in one Singapore cohort about **half of older adults** fell
short of the calcium recommendation [@ye2023calciumSG].

You do **not** need dairy to meet it. **Calcium-set tofu** and **low-oxalate greens** (kai-lan,
bok choy, broccoli) are absorbed *as well as or better than* milk; small fish eaten with their
bones (ikan bilis, sardines) add more. The exception is **spinach** — rich in calcium but
locked up by oxalate, so poorly absorbed [@weaverVegCalcium1999].

![Kai-lan and other low-oxalate leafy greens are good non-dairy sources of calcium, folate and plant iron. Photo: Nthaci, CC BY-SA 4.0.](/figures/food/kai-lan.jpg){#fig-kailan fig-alt="Fresh kai-lan (Chinese broccoli) on a plate." width="300" .lightbox}

::: {.callout-note}
## Does everyone need a calcium supplement? No.
For healthy adults, routine calcium (± vitamin D) supplements have **limited fracture-
prevention benefit**, and some analyses have raised cardiovascular questions
[@uspstfCalcium2018]. The message here is **food-first**: build the bone bank from diet
and activity; reserve supplements for people with genuine shortfall or osteoporosis, under
guidance.
:::

### Iodine — a public-health success worth knowing

Iodine's single job is to build **thyroid hormone**, which sets metabolic rate and — critically
— drives **fetal brain development**. Deficiency causes a swollen thyroid (**goitre**) and, in
pregnancy, **irreversible** intellectual disability in the child; it is a leading *preventable*
cause of brain injury worldwide [@odsIodine]. The fix is one of nutrition's great wins:
**iodising salt**, which has pushed iodine deficiency out of most of the world. The modern
twist to teach is that trendy **non-iodised "gourmet" salts** (sea salt, pink Himalayan) plus
rising plant-based eating (dairy, seafood and eggs are the main sources) can let deficiency
**quietly return** — so keep **iodised salt** as the kitchen default. This matters locally
because salt iodisation in **Singapore is voluntary, not mandatory** [@codling2017iodine], and
no national iodine-status survey has been published — so choosing iodised salt is a sensible
individual safeguard rather than something the food supply guarantees.

![Iodised salt — a cheap population-wide fix for iodine deficiency; most "gourmet" salts are not iodised. Photo: Drtony999, CC BY-SA 3.0.](/figures/food/iodised-salt.jpg){#fig-iodisedsalt fig-alt="A small pile of fine iodised salt on a dark dish." width="300" .lightbox}

### Zinc, magnesium and selenium

**Zinc** supports **immunity, wound healing and growth**; deficiency shows as poor healing, a
rash around the mouth and hands, hair loss and blunted taste — watch vegetarians (the same
phytate story as iron), pregnancy and older adults [@odsZinc]. **Magnesium** is a workhorse
cofactor for nerve, muscle and energy metabolism, drawn from **greens, wholegrains, nuts and
legumes**; low levels track with heavy alcohol use, poorly-controlled diabetes and some
medicines. **Selenium** feeds antioxidant enzymes and thyroid metabolism; deficiency (a heart
muscle disease, *Keshan disease*) depends on **soil** levels, and — a nutrient where **excess is
itself toxic** — a couple of Brazil nuts is plenty [@odsSelenium].

## Water is a nutrient

The body defends its water and electrolyte balance tightly. **Thirst** and the hormone
**ADH** are the everyday regulators, and intake roughly matches losses (urine, skin, breath,
stool). Hot, humid Singapore and exercise **raise** needs [@afn2021curriculum].

There is no magic number of glasses. A simple self-check is **urine colour** — pale straw is
the target. Recognise both ends:

- **Dehydration** — thirst, dark concentrated urine, dizziness, reduced output.
- **Over-hydration** — rarer, but real: drinking *only plain water* in large amounts during
  endurance exercise can cause dangerous **exercise-associated hyponatraemia**. Hydration is
  water **and** electrolytes, not water alone.

## Sodium and potassium: opposite directions

For blood pressure, these two electrolytes pull opposite ways: **too much sodium and too
little potassium raise** blood pressure and cardiovascular risk. WHO targets are **<2,000 mg
sodium/day** [@who2020diet] and **≥3,510 mg potassium/day** [@whoPotassium2012]. Fruit and
vegetables — the half-plate of My Healthy Plate — are the natural potassium source
[@hpbHealthyPlate].

## The Singapore picture

Several threads in this chapter are sharply local — widespread **vitamin D** insufficiency despite
the climate, a **calcium** shortfall in older adults, and iodised salt quietly slipping as "gourmet"
salts spread. But the signature micronutrient issue here runs the other way, an *excess*: **too much
sodium.** The National Nutrition Survey 2022 found average sodium intake around **3,620 mg/day** —
close to **double** the limit — with about **9 in 10** residents exceeding it [@mohNNS2022]. Crucially, most sodium is **not**
the salt-shaker at the table: it hides in **sauces, broths, and food eaten out of home** — a
hawker-culture issue. Some common hawker dishes approach or exceed a *whole day's* sodium in
a single bowl. *(Per-dish figures from consumer-education sources are illustrative and vary
by stall [@shfSaltiestDishes].)*

The population response is policy, not willpower alone. HPB's **"Less Salt, More Taste"**
movement and its work to make **lower-sodium salt, sauces and seasonings** affordable aim to
change the food supply itself [@hpbLessSalt] — the same label-led logic as Nutri-Grade for
sugary drinks [@hpbNutriGrade], and the same idea as iodising salt: **fix the food supply,
not just the individual.**

::: {.callout-warning}
## Salt substitutes work — but read the trial carefully
The SSaSS trial replaced regular salt with a **75% sodium / 25% potassium** substitute and
cut stroke (~14%), major cardiovascular events (~13%) and death (~12%) over ~5 years
[@ssass2021]. **But:** it was **open-label** and **cluster-randomised**, in a **specific
high-risk population** (prior stroke, or older adults with hypertension) in rural China; a
2024 secondary analysis attributes **most** of the blood-pressure fall to the **added
potassium**, not the sodium reduction [@ssassPotassiumBP2024]; and potassium-based
substitutes carry **hyperkalaemia risk** — unsafe in kidney disease or on certain
blood-pressure medications. It is **not** blanket advice. A textbook example of teaching a
trial *with* its caveats.
:::

## So — do I need a supplement?

For most people eating a varied, mostly whole-food diet, no — the recurring answer through this
chapter is **food first.** Micronutrients come packaged in food with the cofactors, fibre and other
nutrients that make them work, and whole-diet patterns beat isolated pills for almost every vitamin
and mineral here. Supplements earn their place for **specific, identifiable needs** — folic acid
before and in early pregnancy, B12 for vegans and many older adults, vitamin D where skin synthesis
is low, iron for a diagnosed deficiency — not as daily insurance against a diet you could improve.
And "more" is not safer: the fat-soluble vitamins and several minerals are **harmful in excess.**
Recognise who genuinely needs what, and reach for the plate before the pillbox.

## Check your understanding

<div data-island="quiz" data-src="/quizzes/micronutrients-hydration.quiz.yml"></div>

## Practise: "tired all the time"

A young woman presents with months of fatigue. Practise recognising the pattern, naming the
at-risk group, and choosing an appropriate action.

::: {.callout-tip}
Progress is saved locally — replay to explore the other paths.
:::

<div data-island="case" data-src="/cases/iron-deficiency-aisha.case.yml"></div>
